title: spectropath from Python
text:
spectropath is an R package. This notebook shows the simplest Python-facing workflow: call the package with Rscript, and then an optional direct bridge with rpy2.
import io
import subprocess
import pandas as pd

title: spectropath from Python
text:
Path-signature features via Rscript

R_CODE = r'''
library(spectropath)

u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

write.csv(path_features(path), stdout(), row.names = FALSE)
'''

result = subprocess.run(
    ['Rscript', '-e', R_CODE],
    check=True,
    capture_output=True,
    text=True,
)

features = pd.read_csv(io.StringIO(result.stdout))
features

title: spectropath from Python
text:
Classical summaries via Rscript

R_CODE = r'''
library(spectropath)

u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

write.csv(classical_features(path), stdout(), row.names = FALSE)
'''

result = subprocess.run(
    ['Rscript', '-e', R_CODE],
    check=True,
    capture_output=True,
    text=True,
)

classical = pd.read_csv(io.StringIO(result.stdout))
classical

title: spectropath from Python
text:
Optional direct bridge with rpy2
Use this only if rpy2 is already installed in your Python environment.

import numpy as np
from rpy2.robjects import r
from rpy2.robjects import numpy2ri

numpy2ri.activate()
r('library(spectropath)')

u = np.linspace(-5, 5, 500)
f = np.exp(-0.5 * u**2) + 0.25 * np.exp(-0.5 * ((u - 1.8) / 0.45)**2)
path = np.column_stack([u, f])

r['path_features'](path)

title: Python
text:
spectropath is an R package. There is not yet a separate native Python package, so the cleanest Python workflow is to call it through Rscript. If you already use rpy2, you can also bridge to the R functions directly.


This keeps the setup simple and works well in notebooks.
import io
import subprocess
import pandas as pd

R_CODE = r'''
library(spectropath)

u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

write.csv(path_features(path), stdout(), row.names = FALSE)
'''

result = subprocess.run(
    ["Rscript", "-e", R_CODE],
    check=True,
    capture_output=True,
    text=True,
)

features = pd.read_csv(io.StringIO(result.stdout))
features

title: Reference
text:
The package keeps the public surface small on purpose.









Function
Purpose




path_features(path, depth = 4)
Return the paper-facing path coordinates


classical_features(path)
Return classical line-profile summaries


as_spectral_path(u, F)
Build a path matrix from ordered coordinate and signal


emabs_area(path)
Compute the emission–absorption ordering coordinate













Output column
Interpretation




p2
signed velocity–flux area


p3u
location of asymmetry along the ordered coordinate


p3F
whether asymmetry is concentrated in bright or faint parts of the line


p4F
higher-order flux modulation


p4T
twist-like higher-order structure


p_pm
emission–absorption ordering



For descriptive names instead of paper notation:
path_features(path, notation = "descriptive")



library(spectropath)

u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

path_features(path)
classical_features(path)



spectropath is the narrow, paper-facing package. It is meant for ordered line-profile diagnostics with stable notation, rather than the broader exploratory feature space that existed in earlier experiments.

title: Reference
text: spectropath is the narrow, paper-facing package. It is meant for ordered line-profile diagnostics with stable notation, rather than the broader exploratory feature space that existed in earlier experiments.

title: spectropath
text:
A small R package for turning spectral lines into named geometric diagnostics. Treat a line profile as an ordered path (X = (u, F)), then compute low-order path-signature coordinates that respond to asymmetry, wings, shoulders, double peaks, and emission–absorption ordering.
Quick start Standalone examples Function reference GitHub



Minimal output
library(spectropath)

u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

path_features(path)
#      p2  p_pm    p3u    p3F    p4F    p4T
# -0.3133 0.000 -0.0169 -0.1736 0.4534 0.0132

title: spectropath
text:
What spectropath returns
The package exposes the paper-facing coordinates and a compact set of classical line-profile summaries for benchmarking.







Output
Interpretation




p2
signed velocity–flux area


p3u
where asymmetry lies along the ordered coordinate


p3F
whether asymmetry is concentrated in bright or faint parts of the line


p4F
higher-order flux modulation


p4T
twist-like higher-order structure


p_pm
emission–absorption ordering

title: spectropath
text:
Quick start
Install from GitHub:
install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("RafaelSdeSouza/spectropath")
library(spectropath)
From a local checkout:
remotes::install_local("/Users/rd23aag/Documents/GitHub/spectropath")
Compute both path and classical summaries from the same line profile:
u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

path_features(path)
classical_features(path)



path_features(path)

title: spectropath
text:
Two visual anchors





Cropped toy-line comparison strip from the current repository figures.






Single-galaxy kinematic crop from the current repository figures.




These are the repository-local assets the site actually uses. The first shows the profile-comparison logic used in the paper discussion of line-shape discrimination. The second is a cropped IFU-facing kinematic example, kept narrower so the homepage stays clean instead of showing the full multi-panel block.
More examples live on the Examples page.
